      "message": "I would pick these three as the most creatively potent: Town API, WhatsApp-native concierge, and the refreshable town-scanner engine. They are related, but each has a different center of gravity: infrastructure, interface, and repeatable machine.

## 1. The Town API: Puerto Viejo as a programmable place

What it looks like as a product:

This is a local intelligence layer, not just `/businesses?category=restaurant`. Think of it as a small but unusually high-context API for one town:

- `/places/search`: "vegan lunch near Cocles with Instagram and WhatsApp"
- `/places/nearby`: lodging-dense area but filtered for laundry, pharmacy, bike rental, breakfast, late-night food
- `/itinerary/blocks`: generate a 3-hour, half-day, rainy-day, kid-safe, no-car, surf-adjacent, Afro-Caribbean food route
- `/business/{id}/contact`: normalized phone, WhatsApp deep link, IG, FB, Booking, TripAdvisor, website
- `/market/gaps`: "areas with 20+ lodging places and fewer than 3 restaurants within walking distance"
- `/visibility/score`: completeness/findability score for each business
- `/changes`: new, closed, renamed, newly social, lost coordinates, changed category after refresh

The product surface could be a developer API, but the more interesting version is an "intent router" for Puerto Viejo. Hotels, hostels, rental villas, bloggers, tour desks, relocation advisors, and AI travel agents can all ask: "where should I send this person right now?" The answer is not generic; it is grounded in the 750-business graph.

Weirdest angle:

Make it a civic substrate: every business becomes an endpoint in the town. A hotel website, a hostel WhatsApp bot, a public kiosk, and a travel creator's guide could all call the same local brain. The weird move is to treat Puerto Viejo like a software platform with routes, dependencies, missing services, freshness checks, and uptime. The town has an API surface.

Even stranger: create "town queries" no global platform optimizes for:

- "I need a place with a real phone number, not just Instagram."
- "What is the most digitally invisible restaurant cluster?"
- "Where can a Spanish-speaking guest book something without needing a website?"
- "Which businesses are likely family-run and not OTA-dominated?"
- "Show places visible on Maps but absent from local directories."

Similar things / comps:

- Google Places API provides establishment and POI data for location-aware features, but it is broad, paid/quotad, and not opinionated around Puerto Viejo-specific travel intents: https://developers.google.com/maps/documentation/places/web-service/overview
- Geoapify Places API exposes category-based POI querying across hundreds of categories: https://www.geoapify.com/places-api/
- Foursquare OS Places is a huge open POI dataset, with 100M+ global POIs and 20+ core attributes, but it is global infrastructure rather than a high-touch local graph: https://opensource.foursquare.com/os-places/
- TravelTime's Google Places alternatives roundup frames this category as POI data for recommendations, reviews, photos, and local search: https://traveltime.com/blog/google-places-api-alternatives-points-of-interest-data
- Placekey is relevant because it treats place identity as infrastructure: a universal identifier for matching/joining physical places across datasets: https://www.placekey.io/

Cheapest MVP:

Do not build a production API first. Build a tiny local "query oracle" over the CSV:

1. Normalize the 750 records into SQLite or DuckDB.
2. Add 20 hand-authored intent tags: rainy_day, walkable_from_center, needs_reservation, WhatsApp_ready, hidden_long_tail, social_first, family_likely, OTA_lodging, no_coordinates, map_only_discovery, etc.
3. Build a single `/ask` endpoint or CLI: user asks natural language, script converts to filters + ranking, returns 5 businesses with contact actions.
4. Create 30 golden queries and manually judge whether the top 5 feel locally intelligent.

The proof is not traffic. The proof is that it answers Puerto Viejo questions better than Google Maps for a constrained set of intents.

## 2. WhatsApp-native concierge: the town speaks in chat

What it looks like as a product:

A traveler messages one number: "We're in Cocles, raining, two kids, no car, want food and something easy after." The concierge replies with 3 options, each with a short explanation and buttons/deep links:

- "Message this restaurant on WhatsApp"
- "Open Instagram"
- "Call"
- "Map it"
- "Ask in Spanish"

The core product is not an AI travel planner. It is a contact bridge. It should shorten the distance between visitor intent and local business conversation.

Important product details:

- Bilingual by default, with local phrasing.
- Generates prefilled WhatsApp messages in Spanish: "Hola, somos 2 adultos y 2 ninos, estamos cerca de Cocles. Estan abiertos hoy?"
- Uses fallback channels intelligently: if no WhatsApp, phone; if no phone, IG; if lodging, Booking link; if tour, FB/website.
- Has modes: "near me", "open-ish now" if hours exist later, "low friction", "local/less touristy", "rain plan", "no car", "family", "date night", "under $".
- Can be embedded by hotels: "Ask Paradisio" QR card at reception or in room.
- Can produce a concierge handoff log: what guests asked for, which businesses got referred, which categories are missing.

Weirdest angle:

Do not try to complete bookings inside the app. Make the product intentionally end at the threshold of human conversation. In a place like Puerto Viejo, the living network matters more than sterile booking flows. The concierge becomes a "message composer for place-based trust."

Even weirder: sell it as a town-wide switchboard where businesses can subscribe to get better pre-qualified inbound messages. Instead of ads, they receive better context: party size, language, location, urgency, needs. The dataset becomes the routing table for human-to-human commerce.

Similar things / comps:

- GuideGeek is a WhatsApp/Facebook/Instagram travel chatbot, positioned as a travel concierge in your pocket: https://matadornetwork.com/read/ai-trip-planning/
- Hotel and destination operators are increasingly using WhatsApp for pre-arrival coordination, guest requests, service updates, and upsells: https://www.hotelnewsresource.com/article138854.html
- Runnr describes AI-powered WhatsApp tools acting as virtual concierges for hotel guests and reducing front-desk load: https://runnr.ai/blog/using-whatsapp-for-automated-guest-messaging-in-hospitality
- Landbot lists WhatsApp travel/hospitality use cases like local transport advice, avoiding tourist traps, restaurant reservations, room service, and airport rides: https://landbot.io/blog/whatsapp-travel-hospitality-use-cases
- Simbas offers branded AI concierge on website and WhatsApp for travel businesses, with support for partner answers and team handoff: https://simbas.ai/features/ai-external-chat
- Naurra has a case study for a Telegram/WhatsApp AI travel concierge generating itineraries and reducing itinerary creation time: https://naurra.ai/case-studies/ai-travel-concierge-whatsapp-telegram-itineraries/

Cheapest MVP:

Avoid WhatsApp Business API at first. Build a web page that behaves like a WhatsApp launcher:

1. A mobile-first page: "What do you need in Puerto Viejo?"
2. 12 intent buttons plus free text.
3. Query the CSV locally and return 3 recommended businesses.
4. Each result has `wa.me` links or phone/IG fallback.
5. The generated Spanish message is visible and editable before opening WhatsApp.
6. Put QR codes in 2-3 friendly hostels/hotels or send to 20 travelers.
7. Track only simple events: query, result clicked, contact channel used.

MVP success metric: 30 real visitors use it and at least 10 click through to contact a business. The question is whether the dataset can route intent into local conversations.

## 3. Refreshable town-scanner engine: the portable commercial census machine

What it looks like as a product:

This is the most defensible idea if the pipeline is the asset. Productize the method that produced Puerto Viejo:

"Give us a tourism town. We return a living commercial graph in 48 hours."

Output package:

- Master CSV / SQLite / API of businesses within a radius
- Confidence + provenance per field
- Coverage report: Maps, OSM, local directories, websites, Instagram, WhatsApp, Booking/TripAdvisor
- Gap report: missing coordinates, missing categories, no phone, no social, no web, likely duplicates
- Change report on refresh: new, gone, renamed, contact changed, social added/removed
- Opportunity map: service gaps, digital presence gaps, lodging/restaurant imbalance, invisible clusters

The product could target:

- Destination marketing organizations
- Boutique hotel groups entering a town
- Travel media companies
- Local SEO agencies
- Relocation guides
- Market researchers
- Tour operators building partner networks
- Entrepreneurs evaluating where to open something

Weirdest angle:

The product is not "scraping." It is a colonoscopy for a town's commercial internet presence. It finds what the global platforms see, what they miss, and what local businesses forgot to connect.

The strangest, strongest version: sell "freshness" rather than data. A static CSV decays. A monthly pulse becomes a signal: who opened, who died, who got serious about Instagram, who joined Booking, which stretch of road is becoming more commercial, where tourism pressure is creeping.

Another weird angle: use the deltas as a cultural early-warning system. In a small tourism town, new yoga studios, surf schools, boutique stays, and English-first Instagram bios are signals of neighborhood change before official data catches up.

Similar things / comps:

- All the Places is an open data project using scrapers to produce standardized POI data from store-location pages; its public site says it indexes 20M+ POIs from 4,100+ spiders and updates weekly: https://alltheplaces.xyz/ and https://github.com/alltheplaces/alltheplaces
- Apify's Google Maps Scraper positions extracted Maps business data for lead generation, competitor analysis, and growth workflows: https://apify.com/compass/crawler-google-places
- Apify's OSM POI extractor explicitly mentions periodic refresh pipelines for dashboards, reports, and alerting, plus market-gap and saturation analysis: https://apify.com/automation-lab/openstreetmap-poi-extractor
- Outscraper frames Google Maps enrichment as replacing manual research with structured business names, addresses, phones, websites, and Place IDs: https://outscraper.com/google-maps-places-data-enrichment
- Openmart sells local-business enrichment with social profiles, direct phones, owner emails, tech stacks, and many structured fields: https://www.openmart.com/products/local-business-enrichment
- OSM community discussions show the hard part: keeping POI data complete and current, especially opening hours and business changes, remains a persistent need: https://community.openstreetmap.org/t/automatically-update-opening-hours-of-businesses/92813

Cheapest MVP:

Run the exact method on a second comparable town, but make the deliverable the product:

1. Pick one town: Cahuita, Santa Teresa, Nosara, La Fortuna, or Bocas.
2. Limit scope to 300-500 businesses within a radius.
3. Produce the same unified schema plus coverage metrics.
4. Create a two-town comparison memo: category mix, contact completeness, platform dependence, IG penetration, Booking dependence, phone/WhatsApp availability, invisible businesses.
5. Build one static dashboard with filters and before/after-style methodology notes.

MVP success metric: someone who knows the second town says, "This is weirdly complete," and can name 10 useful actions from it.

The key test is portability. If Puerto Viejo was a one-off heroic weekend, it is a project. If the second town works, it is a machine.

## Fourth idea, off the wall: The Puerto Viejo Economic Tarot / living oracle

This is not a normal SaaS product. It is a playful, eerie, data-driven artifact: a "town oracle" that uses the business graph to tell stories, predict moods, and generate rituals of exploration.

What it could be:

A visitor or local draws three cards:

- Past: an old-school soda, cabina, or service business
- Present: a high-visibility Instagram/Booking-era business
- Future: a map-only discovery, category gap, or business with weak digital presence but strong location

Each reading produces a walk, a meal, a conversation prompt, and a micro-economic observation about the town. Example:

"You drew Laundry, Surf School, and Hidden Cabina. Today Puerto Viejo is telling you to follow the support economy, not the tourist economy. Walk from lodging density toward the services that keep the town functioning. Message this soda in Spanish. Ask this shop what changed after 2020."

Why the dataset enables it:

The data has enough categories, contact channels, visibility gaps, platform links, and coordinates to create symbolic archetypes:

- The Ghost: business visible in one source but absent elsewhere
- The Beacon: complete digital presence, IG + Maps + phone + website
- The Whisper: no website, maybe phone only
- The Portal: WhatsApp-ready business
- The Mask: tourist-facing brand with little local metadata
- The Root: services locals need, not just visitors
- The Tide: lodging cluster pulling restaurants/shops around it

This could become:

- A weird travel guide
- A data-art website
- A deck of physical cards sold locally
- A daily "town reading" Instagram account
- A walking-tour generator
- A way to make invisible local commerce emotionally legible

      "message": "I would pick these three as the most creatively potent: Town API, WhatsApp-native concierge, and the refreshable town-scanner engine. They are related, but each has a different center of gravity: infrastructure, interface, and repeatable machine.

## 1. The Town API: Puerto Viejo as a programmable place

What it looks like as a product:

This is a local intelligence layer, not just `/businesses?category=restaurant`. Think of it as a small but unusually high-context API for one town:

- `/places/search`: "vegan lunch near Cocles with Instagram and WhatsApp"
- `/places/nearby`: lodging-dense area but filtered for laundry, pharmacy, bike rental, breakfast, late-night food
- `/itinerary/blocks`: generate a 3-hour, half-day, rainy-day, kid-safe, no-car, surf-adjacent, Afro-Caribbean food route
- `/business/{id}/contact`: normalized phone, WhatsApp deep link, IG, FB, Booking, TripAdvisor, website
- `/market/gaps`: "areas with 20+ lodging places and fewer than 3 restaurants within walking distance"
- `/visibility/score`: completeness/findability score for each business
- `/changes`: new, closed, renamed, newly social, lost coordinates, changed category after refresh

The product surface could be a developer API, but the more interesting version is an "intent router" for Puerto Viejo. Hotels, hostels, rental villas, bloggers, tour desks, relocation advisors, and AI travel agents can all ask: "where should I send this person right now?" The answer is not generic; it is grounded in the 750-business graph.

Weirdest angle:

Make it a civic substrate: every business becomes an endpoint in the town. A hotel website, a hostel WhatsApp bot, a public kiosk, and a travel creator's guide could all call the same local brain. The weird move is to treat Puerto Viejo like a software platform with routes, dependencies, missing services, freshness checks, and uptime. The town has an API surface.

Even stranger: create "town queries" no global platform optimizes for:

- "I need a place with a real phone number, not just Instagram."
- "What is the most digitally invisible restaurant cluster?"
- "Where can a Spanish-speaking guest book something without needing a website?"
- "Which businesses are likely family-run and not OTA-dominated?"
- "Show places visible on Maps but absent from local directories."

Similar things / comps:

- Google Places API provides establishment and POI data for location-aware features, but it is broad, paid/quotad, and not opinionated around Puerto Viejo-specific travel intents: https://developers.google.com/maps/documentation/places/web-service/overview
- Geoapify Places API exposes category-based POI querying across hundreds of categories: https://www.geoapify.com/places-api/
- Foursquare OS Places is a huge open POI dataset, with 100M+ global POIs and 20+ core attributes, but it is global infrastructure rather than a high-touch local graph: https://opensource.foursquare.com/os-places/
- TravelTime's Google Places alternatives roundup frames this category as POI data for recommendations, reviews, photos, and local search: https://traveltime.com/blog/google-places-api-alternatives-points-of-interest-data
- Placekey is relevant because it treats place identity as infrastructure: a universal identifier for matching/joining physical places across datasets: https://www.placekey.io/

Cheapest MVP:

Do not build a production API first. Build a tiny local "query oracle" over the CSV:

1. Normalize the 750 records into SQLite or DuckDB.
2. Add 20 hand-authored intent tags: rainy_day, walkable_from_center, needs_reservation, WhatsApp_ready, hidden_long_tail, social_first, family_likely, OTA_lodging, no_coordinates, map_only_discovery, etc.
3. Build a single `/ask` endpoint or CLI: user asks natural language, script converts to filters + ranking, returns 5 businesses with contact actions.
4. Create 30 golden queries and manually judge whether the top 5 feel locally intelligent.

The proof is not traffic. The proof is that it answers Puerto Viejo questions better than Google Maps for a constrained set of intents.

## 2. WhatsApp-native concierge: the town speaks in chat

What it looks like as a product:

A traveler messages one number: "We're in Cocles, raining, two kids, no car, want food and something easy after." The concierge replies with 3 options, each with a short explanation and buttons/deep links:

- "Message this restaurant on WhatsApp"
- "Open Instagram"
- "Call"
- "Map it"
- "Ask in Spanish"

The core product is not an AI travel planner. It is a contact bridge. It should shorten the distance between visitor intent and local business conversation.

Important product details:

- Bilingual by default, with local phrasing.
- Generates prefilled WhatsApp messages in Spanish: "Hola, somos 2 adultos y 2 ninos, estamos cerca de Cocles. Estan abiertos hoy?"
- Uses fallback channels intelligently: if no WhatsApp, phone; if no phone, IG; if lodging, Booking link; if tour, FB/website.
- Has modes: "near me", "open-ish now" if hours exist later, "low friction", "local/less touristy", "rain plan", "no car", "family", "date night", "under $".
- Can be embedded by hotels: "Ask Paradisio" QR card at reception or in room.
- Can produce a concierge handoff log: what guests asked for, which businesses got referred, which categories are missing.

Weirdest angle:

Do not try to complete bookings inside the app. Make the product intentionally end at the threshold of human conversation. In a place like Puerto Viejo, the living network matters more than sterile booking flows. The concierge becomes a "message composer for place-based trust."

Even weirder: sell it as a town-wide switchboard where businesses can subscribe to get better pre-qualified inbound messages. Instead of ads, they receive better context: party size, language, location, urgency, needs. The dataset becomes the routing table for human-to-human commerce.

Similar things / comps:

- GuideGeek is a WhatsApp/Facebook/Instagram travel chatbot, positioned as a travel concierge in your pocket: https://matadornetwork.com/read/ai-trip-planning/
- Hotel and destination operators are increasingly using WhatsApp for pre-arrival coordination, guest requests, service updates, and upsells: https://www.hotelnewsresource.com/article138854.html
- Runnr describes AI-powered WhatsApp tools acting as virtual concierges for hotel guests and reducing front-desk load: https://runnr.ai/blog/using-whatsapp-for-automated-guest-messaging-in-hospitality
- Landbot lists WhatsApp travel/hospitality use cases like local transport advice, avoiding tourist traps, restaurant reservations, room service, and airport rides: https://landbot.io/blog/whatsapp-travel-hospitality-use-cases
- Simbas offers branded AI concierge on website and WhatsApp for travel businesses, with support for partner answers and team handoff: https://simbas.ai/features/ai-external-chat
- Naurra has a case study for a Telegram/WhatsApp AI travel concierge generating itineraries and reducing itinerary creation time: https://naurra.ai/case-studies/ai-travel-concierge-whatsapp-telegram-itineraries/

Cheapest MVP:

Avoid WhatsApp Business API at first. Build a web page that behaves like a WhatsApp launcher:

1. A mobile-first page: "What do you need in Puerto Viejo?"
2. 12 intent buttons plus free text.
3. Query the CSV locally and return 3 recommended businesses.
4. Each result has `wa.me` links or phone/IG fallback.
5. The generated Spanish message is visible and editable before opening WhatsApp.
6. Put QR codes in 2-3 friendly hostels/hotels or send to 20 travelers.
7. Track only simple events: query, result clicked, contact channel used.

MVP success metric: 30 real visitors use it and at least 10 click through to contact a business. The question is whether the dataset can route intent into local conversations.

## 3. Refreshable town-scanner engine: the portable commercial census machine

What it looks like as a product:

This is the most defensible idea if the pipeline is the asset. Productize the method that produced Puerto Viejo:

"Give us a tourism town. We return a living commercial graph in 48 hours."

Output package:

- Master CSV / SQLite / API of businesses within a radius
- Confidence + provenance per field
- Coverage report: Maps, OSM, local directories, websites, Instagram, WhatsApp, Booking/TripAdvisor
- Gap report: missing coordinates, missing categories, no phone, no social, no web, likely duplicates
- Change report on refresh: new, gone, renamed, contact changed, social added/removed
- Opportunity map: service gaps, digital presence gaps, lodging/restaurant imbalance, invisible clusters

The product could target:

- Destination marketing organizations
- Boutique hotel groups entering a town
- Travel media companies
- Local SEO agencies
- Relocation guides
- Market researchers
- Tour operators building partner networks
- Entrepreneurs evaluating where to open something

Weirdest angle:

The product is not "scraping." It is a colonoscopy for a town's commercial internet presence. It finds what the global platforms see, what they miss, and what local businesses forgot to connect.

The strangest, strongest version: sell "freshness" rather than data. A static CSV decays. A monthly pulse becomes a signal: who opened, who died, who got serious about Instagram, who joined Booking, which stretch of road is becoming more commercial, where tourism pressure is creeping.

Another weird angle: use the deltas as a cultural early-warning system. In a small tourism town, new yoga studios, surf schools, boutique stays, and English-first Instagram bios are signals of neighborhood change before official data catches up.

Similar things / comps:

- All the Places is an open data project using scrapers to produce standardized POI data from store-location pages; its public site says it indexes 20M+ POIs from 4,100+ spiders and updates weekly: https://alltheplaces.xyz/ and https://github.com/alltheplaces/alltheplaces
- Apify's Google Maps Scraper positions extracted Maps business data for lead generation, competitor analysis, and growth workflows: https://apify.com/compass/crawler-google-places
- Apify's OSM POI extractor explicitly mentions periodic refresh pipelines for dashboards, reports, and alerting, plus market-gap and saturation analysis: https://apify.com/automation-lab/openstreetmap-poi-extractor
- Outscraper frames Google Maps enrichment as replacing manual research with structured business names, addresses, phones, websites, and Place IDs: https://outscraper.com/google-maps-places-data-enrichment
- Openmart sells local-business enrichment with social profiles, direct phones, owner emails, tech stacks, and many structured fields: https://www.openmart.com/products/local-business-enrichment
- OSM community discussions show the hard part: keeping POI data complete and current, especially opening hours and business changes, remains a persistent need: https://community.openstreetmap.org/t/automatically-update-opening-hours-of-businesses/92813

Cheapest MVP:

Run the exact method on a second comparable town, but make the deliverable the product:

1. Pick one town: Cahuita, Santa Teresa, Nosara, La Fortuna, or Bocas.
2. Limit scope to 300-500 businesses within a radius.
3. Produce the same unified schema plus coverage metrics.
4. Create a two-town comparison memo: category mix, contact completeness, platform dependence, IG penetration, Booking dependence, phone/WhatsApp availability, invisible businesses.
5. Build one static dashboard with filters and before/after-style methodology notes.

MVP success metric: someone who knows the second town says, "This is weirdly complete," and can name 10 useful actions from it.

The key test is portability. If Puerto Viejo was a one-off heroic weekend, it is a project. If the second town works, it is a machine.

## Fourth idea, off the wall: The Puerto Viejo Economic Tarot / living oracle

This is not a normal SaaS product. It is a playful, eerie, data-driven artifact: a "town oracle" that uses the business graph to tell stories, predict moods, and generate rituals of exploration.

What it could be:

A visitor or local draws three cards:

- Past: an old-school soda, cabina, or service business
- Present: a high-visibility Instagram/Booking-era business
- Future: a map-only discovery, category gap, or business with weak digital presence but strong location

Each reading produces a walk, a meal, a conversation prompt, and a micro-economic observation about the town. Example:

"You drew Laundry, Surf School, and Hidden Cabina. Today Puerto Viejo is telling you to follow the support economy, not the tourist economy. Walk from lodging density toward the services that keep the town functioning. Message this soda in Spanish. Ask this shop what changed after 2020."

Why the dataset enables it:

The data has enough categories, contact channels, visibility gaps, platform links, and coordinates to create symbolic archetypes:

- The Ghost: business visible in one source but absent elsewhere
- The Beacon: complete digital presence, IG + Maps + phone + website
- The Whisper: no website, maybe phone only
- The Portal: WhatsApp-ready business
- The Mask: tourist-facing brand with little local metadata
- The Root: services locals need, not just visitors
- The Tide: lodging cluster pulling restaurants/shops around it

This could become:

- A weird travel guide
- A data-art website
- A deck of physical cards sold locally
- A daily "town reading" Instagram account
- A walking-tour generator
- A way to make invisible local commerce emotionally legible

# Paradisio — The Puerto Viejo Operating System

## Thesis

A persistent web application that is the town's commercial and cultural operating system. Every business is listed by default (opt-out, not opt-in). Every local artist, photographer, musician, poet, and creator has a profile alongside them. The whole thing is fed by a live Instagram capture of 452+ handles, a QR code affiliate network, a quarterly print magazine, a middleman service layer, and AI-augmented upsells. It is Craigslist for a single town, but reversed — everyone is already on it, and we sell them premium tools to own their presence.

---

## Core Principles

1. **Opt-out, not opt-in.** The 750 businesses are already listed with their data. The burden is on them to leave, not to join.
2. **Persistent web app.** One URL. Works on any device. No app store. The town's surface.
3. **Craigslist aesthetic, not SaaS.** Text-first, fast, ugly in a way that signals authenticity. Works on a $30 smartphone in 2G.
4. **Local contact layer, not a booking engine.** The transaction happens on WhatsApp. We route intent into conversation.
5. **Physical + digital hybrid.** QR codes on doors, a quarterly magazine in lobbies, and a live web app behind it all.
6. **The dataset is the moat.** 750 businesses, 452 IG handles, 611 phones, 699 CIDs, 361 Facebook URLs, 191 websites, 106 WhatsApps, 75 emails — all in one normalized graph. Nobody else has this.

---

## Data Sources

| Source | Records | Key fields |
|--------|---------|------------|
| PV Satellite crawl | 450 | Names, categories, areas, coordinates, CIDs, phone, IG, FB, Booking, TripAdvisor |
| OSM cross-reference | +31 | Coordinates, phone, website, OSM tags |
| Maps grid scan (Playwright + vision) | +134 | Business names discovered via screenshots |
| PVS SQLite cache | +139 | Additional contact data |
| Website crawl | 81 sites | IG, FB, email, WhatsApp from 191 websites |
| Instagram verification (Playwright) | 452 verified | Working IG handles with confidence scores |
| Stealth Maps search | 265 resolved | CIDs, coords, phone, website |
| **Unified master** | **750** | **34 columns per record** |

---

## Products & Revenue

### Tier 1 — Premium Listing ($100/yr year 1, $200/yr year 2)

What the business gets:
- A permanent page on the platform with their data, photos, contact channels, and map pin
- A physical QR code sticker for their door/window (SINPE trackable)
- Featured placement in their category and neighborhood
- Monthly scan/view analytics
- Auto-generated business page (AI takes their existing data and produces a one-page site)

The $100 is not revenue — it is a commitment mechanism. Year 1 hooks them. Year 2 is a renewal they'll pay because by then the platform is generating inbound.

### Tier 2 — AI Service Add-ons ($10-30/mo each)

| Service | Description | Price |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| WhatsApp auto-reply | AI answers common questions (hours, menu, prices, location) in Spanish/English | $20/mo |
| IG content pack | 4 AI-generated posts/month from their business data | $30/mo |
| Menu/price translation | Spanish/English/French translation of menu or service list | $50 one-time |
| Review response drafts | Auto-generate replies to TripAdvisor/Google reviews | $15/mo |
| Booking link integration | Connect Booking.com/Airbnb calendar to their page | $10/mo |
| Photo refresh | AI-enhanced or commissioned photography of their space | $75 one-time |

### Tier 3 — Middleman Service Fee (10-20% per transaction)

The platform intermediates specific service bookings:

| Category | Service | Our cut |
|----------|---------|---------|
| Transport | Airport shuttle, van driver SJ↔PV, taxi, boat transfer | 10-15% |
| Tours | Snorkeling, sloth sanctuary, chocolate, night walk, ATV | 10-20% |
| Dining | Reservation with pre-order, chef's table coordination | 5-10% |
| Activities | Surf lessons, yoga, bike rental, photography session | 10-15% |
| Services | Massage, laundry, bike repair, Spanish tutor, medical | 15-20% |

The platform confirms both sides, sends reminders, handles bilingual coordination, and manages fallback. The transaction happens on WhatsApp + SINPE. We take our cut.

---

## The Affiliate QR Code Network

### How it works

Local salespeople (students, surf instructors, bartenders, ticos) walk the town. They already know the business owners. They say:

> *"Your restaurant is already on the Puerto Viejo board. 50 people saw your page this month. Put this QR code on your door and every tourist Instagramming their food can message you directly. First year, we do sticker + setup — 10 mil colones (SINPE)."*

### QR code tiers

| Variant | What it does | Price |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| Basic | Opens listing page (WhatsApp, IG, phone, map) | $100/yr |
| Menu | Opens menu + WhatsApp | $150/yr |
| Booking | Opens Booking/Airbnb page + WhatsApp | $200/yr |
| Review | Opens TripAdvisor/Google review prompt | $50/yr add-on |

Every scan is trackable. After 6 months: *"Your QR code got scanned 300 times. 45 people opened WhatsApp. You paid 10mil. That's 22mil per new conversation."*

### Affiliate commission

Commission on each QR code sale. Paid instantly via SINPE. The affiliate network is the sales force. No salaries, no benefits, no overhead.

---

## The Instagram Capture Engine

### Live aggregation

452 verified Instagram handles. Every post, every story, every hashtag. Captured continuously.

### What it feeds

| Output | What it does |
|--------|-------------|
| Event detection | 3 hotels IG'd the same thing = there's a festival, wedding, retreat |
| Dinner rush signal | Restaurants posting specials in real time |
| Beach/conditions | Surf schools posting wave pics = surf report |
| Dead zone detection | Areas with no posts this week = invisible businesses |
| Hashtag analytics | #PuertoViejo, #Cocles, #Cahuita, #PuntaUva, #CaribbeanCostaRica trending |
| Magazine content | Aggregated IG data becomes editorial |

### Business-facing dashboard

- Your post reach this month
- Your hashtag performance vs. competitors
- Best times to post by category
- "You're the most Instagrammed coffee shop in Cocles"

---

## The Quarterly Magazine

A beautiful, high-gloss, bilingual printed magazine. Distributed to every hotel lobby, hostel common room, rental villa, tour desk, and bar in town.

### Content sourced from the data stream

- "The 20 Most Instagrammed Tables in Puerto Viejo This Season"
- "What Cocles Ate in July" — aggregated from restaurant Instagrams
- "Wave Report" — surf spots ranked by IG story frequency
- "The Quiet Places" — businesses with amazing service but zero social presence
- "New in Town" — detected from new Instagram accounts appearing in the area
- Full-page premium member ads
- QR code on every page → deep link to the business on the platform

### Why businesses host it

It makes their lobby feel curated. They don't realize they're also distributing a sales catalog that trains every tourist to use the platform. The FOMO is built in: businesses that didn't pay see their name next to ones that did.

---

## The Creative Layer

### Profiles alongside businesses

The platform isn't just hotels and restaurants. It includes:

- Local photographers
- Musicians and bands
- Poets and writers
- Muralists and visual artists
- Surf instructors (as performers, not just services)
- Chefs (as artists, not just restaurant listings)
- Models and content creators
- International artists who want to collaborate

### Why it matters

The creative layer is the content engine that makes the business board *interesting.* A tourist doesn't just look for a hotel — they look for what's happening tonight. The artists posting their gigs, the photographer offering a sunset shoot, the poet doing a reading at the hostel bar — this is what makes a town feel alive.

### Cross-listing

> *"You're a bar. Your musician plays here every Thursday. We cross-list them. Their fans find you, your guests find them."*

---

## The Community Layer

| Surface | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| Reddit (r/PuertoViejo) | Visitor questions, trip reports, "is this place still good?" |
| Discord | Real-time chat: live music tonight, surf conditions, rideshares, event chatter |
| Blog | Long-form profiles, deep interviews, photo essays, history pieces |
| Magazine | Quarterly print version of the best content |

All populated from the same data stream. Instagram capture feeds the blog. Event detection feeds the Discord. Business listings feed the subreddit wiki. Magazine content gets republished as blog posts.

---

## The Flywheel

```
Instagram posts → aggregated analytics & event detection
  → magazine editorial + real-time web app updates
    → physical distribution + QR code scans
      → tourist uses platform
        → business gets booked
          → business upgrades to premium
            → more Instagram posts
```

Every quarter, the magazine is the physical artifact proving the network is alive.

---

## Pricing Summary

| Product | Year 1 | Year 2 |
|---------|--------|--------|
| Premium listing + QR sticker | $100 | $200 |
| AI add-ons (per month) | $10-30 | $10-30 |
| Middleman transaction | 10-20% | 10-20% |
| Magazine full-page spread | $150 | $250 |
| Affiliate commission | 20-40% of sale | 20-40% of sale |

---

## The One-Page Pitch

> **Paradisio is the default operating system for Puerto Viejo commerce and culture.**
>
> Every business and artist in town is already listed. QR codes on every door route tourists into WhatsApp conversations. A live Instagram engine detects events, trends, and dead zones. A quarterly magazine proves the network is real. AI augments every business's digital presence for a fraction of what a website costs.
>
> Year 1 is $100 to own your page. Year 2 is $200. The sticker on your door never stops working. The platform never stops feeding you inbound. Opt out if you want — but why would you?

---

*Built on the Puerto Viejo Business Discovery dataset — 750 businesses, 452 IG handles, 611 phones, 699 CIDs. Zero API costs. Full audit trail.*
